# VLM-Based OCR: Evaluating New Approaches

## Overview

Vision Language Models (VLMs) offer a fundamentally different approach to OCR compared to traditional methods. This chapter evaluates when and how VLMs might complement or replace existing OCR workflows in GLAM institutions.

## Key Questions This Chapter Addresses

- When is it worth re-OCRing existing collections with VLMs?
- How do VLMs compare to traditional OCR in accuracy, cost, and outputs?
- What new capabilities do VLMs enable beyond text extraction?
- How can VLM-extracted text better support downstream AI tasks?

## Evaluation Framework

### Performance Metrics
- **Accuracy**: Character and word error rates across different document types
- **Cost**: Per-page processing costs vs traditional OCR
- **Speed**: Processing time for different collection sizes
- **Output Quality**: Readability and structure preservation

### Output Format Comparison

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Traditional OCR (ALTO XML)
- Preserves exact page coordinates
- Industry standard for digital libraries
- Complex structure, harder to process
- Limited semantic understanding

### VLM Output (Markdown)
- Human-readable format
- Preserves document structure semantically
- Easier integration with LLMs
- Better for full-text search and RAG

### Hybrid Approaches
- Use VLMs for complex layouts
- Traditional OCR for simple text
- Combine coordinate and semantic data

:::

## When to Consider Re-OCRing

::: {.callout-tip}
## Decision Framework
Consider VLM-based re-OCRing when:
- Existing OCR is >10 years old
- You need better handling of complex layouts
- Downstream AI applications require clean text
- Cost per page is acceptable for your budget
:::

## Enabling Downstream AI Tasks

### RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)
- Cleaner text improves retrieval accuracy
- Markdown format works better with embeddings
- Structural understanding enhances context

### Fine-tuning and Training
- Higher quality training data from historical texts
- Better preservation of document semantics
- Reduced preprocessing requirements

### Semantic Search
- Improved indexing with better text quality
- Enhanced metadata extraction
- Cross-collection discovery

## Case Studies

### National Library Examples
- Newspaper collections with complex layouts
- Handwritten materials mixed with print
- Multi-language collections
- Scientific texts with formulas and diagrams

## Cost-Benefit Analysis

[To be developed with specific pricing models and benchmarks]

## Tools and Resources

- Evaluation scripts for comparing OCR outputs
- Sample datasets for benchmarking
- Cost calculators for different scales
- Integration guides for existing workflows

## Next Steps

1. Pilot evaluation on representative samples
2. Benchmark against existing OCR
3. Calculate ROI for full collection processing
4. Develop integration strategy
